preFix.title: Building a simple tool cabinet with a plywood back
preFix.subtitle: Make a shallow wall-hung cabinet from timber and plywood for workshop tool storage
preFix.excerpt: How to build a shallow wall-hung tool cabinet from PAR timber sides and shelves with a 9 mm plywood back, covering cutting to length, gluing and screwing the carcass, fitting the plywood back, and hanging it from a batten.
preFix.sourceNotes: Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry and Joinery. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics (cabinet-making). Carpentry for Boys (1914). Collins Complete DIY Manual.

content.text: A simple tool cabinet is one of the first useful woodworking projects for a home workshop. The
carcass
is
PAR timber
sides, top, and bottom with a 9 mm plywood back glued and screwed in place. The plywood back makes the carcass rigid and square. A
French cleat
at the back allows the cabinet to hang from the wall and be repositioned without re-drilling.
What you will need: materials

For a cabinet 800 mm wide x 600 mm tall x 150 mm deep: two side pieces at 600 x 150 mm from 18 mm PAR timber, one top and one bottom at 764 x 150 mm (to fit between the sides), and one or two internal shelves at the same 764 mm length. A sheet of 9 mm ply at 800 x 600 mm for the back. Two lengths of PAR at 800 mm for the French cleat (one on the wall, one on the cabinet). No. 8 x 38 mm and No. 8 x 25 mm screws. PVA wood glue.
Step 1, cut the carcass parts to length

Mark each piece with the carpenters pencil and the combination square, checking the line at 90 degrees to the face. Cut the sides and top/bottom pieces to length with the crosscut saw. Accuracy at this stage sets the squareness of the whole cabinet. After cutting, check each piece for square at the ends: a parallelogram cut on the top piece means the cabinet cannot be made square. Sand the end-grain surfaces smooth with the sanding block so they mate cleanly when joined.
Step 2, assemble the carcass

Apply PVA to the end grain of the top piece and clamp it between the two sides, flush at the front face. Pre-drill and countersink a screw hole through the side into the end of the top piece: two No. 8 x 38 mm screws per corner. Drive the screws to pull the joint tight. Repeat at the bottom. Check the carcass for square by measuring the two diagonals: they must be equal within 1 mm. Adjust the cramps if needed before the glue sets.
Step 3, fit the plywood back

Cut the plywood back to exactly match the outer dimension of the carcass. Check it for square before gluing: a square back glued to a slightly racked carcass squares the carcass; a racked back fixes the rack permanently. Apply a bead of PVA around the back edge of the carcass, position the plywood back, and drive 25 mm screws at 150 mm centres around the perimeter. Check the carcass for square again immediately: the plywood should have pulled it to square.
Step 4, cut the French cleat and hang the cabinet

Rip two 800 mm lengths of 75 x 25 mm PAR along a 45-degree bevel. A hand saw at 45 degrees with a mitre box works for short lengths; a ripping cut along the length requires careful control of the saw. Fix one piece to the cabinet back with the bevel face-down, about 75 mm from the top. Fix the other to the wall at the intended hanging height with the bevel face-up. The cabinet hooks over the wall piece and hangs on the bevel.
Variations

Add a door by hinging two plywood panels (one each side) to the front of the cabinet on piano hinge. A magnetic catch keeps each panel closed. For a larger cabinet, increase the wall cleat length and use two cabinet pieces side by side on the same cleat system. The cleat allows any number of cabinets to be arranged and rearranged on the wall.
Care

Check the wall fixing screws of the cleat annually for any sign of movement: a cabinet loaded with heavy tools exerts a significant pull on the wall fixing. Redecorate the interior with a paint or varnish if rust from tools begins to mark the timber shelves.

attrs.fix: Check the back with the try square before fitting. If the cabinet is already assembled and racked, remove the back, square the carcass by adjusting the cramp positions, and refit the back.
attrs.cause: Carcass racked before the back was fitted; or the back itself was cut out of square.
attrs.symptom: Cabinet is not square after assembly: door gap is uneven

attrs.fix: Drill a pilot hole slightly smaller than the screw shank before re-driving. In end grain, screws have weaker grip than in face grain: two screws per corner give adequate strength.
attrs.cause: No pre-drilled pilot hole; screw is splitting the end grain rather than gripping.
attrs.symptom: Screws through the side pull into the end grain of the top and do not grip

attrs.fix: The two pieces must be ripped at the same angle. Recut one piece to match the other. A good French cleat drops onto the wall cleat and sits completely stable without rattling.
attrs.cause: Cabinet cleat bevel is too steep or the wall cleat bevel is too shallow; the two bevels are not matching angles.
attrs.symptom: Cabinet rocks on the French cleat wall piece

PATTERN tutorial has no Method/Steps section and no orderedList — sequential instructions missing
